entries.form_section: Syr. a/e but generally a/u in Jewish texts. In JBA pael often without a spelled final nun. In JBA prints peal often with thematic i vowel -- surely erroneously; Nab. T stem without dental-sibilant metathesis.
entries.end_notes:
For the ultimate etymology of this uniquely Aramaic verb see discussion in AIOA pp. 112f.
Note that effectively it is very difficult to distinguish between Dt and Gt in unvocalized texts; perhaps it is better simply to render either as "to be subjected to a purchase transaction". Explicit examples of Gt, however, appear to be quite rare.

entries.end_notes:
Purchase and "sale" are two different words in many languages; in Aramaic zbn can be used for either perspective, and there is little reason to assume (cf. DNWSI) that there might be different noun-formations involved in early dialects. By the time of Syriac, however, given the existence of the explicit D v.n zwbn, this form might be limited to the idea of "buying."
However, the number of noun formations actually attested is uncertain; see s.v. zbn#3 n.m.

In SamTgJ Gen 37:19, 24:65 זעיה renders Hebrew הלזה as a phonetic and midrashic play, taking it as if from the root ˁlz; see Tal under the peal finite form.
The gloss "careful, wary" in LS2 is one of the very finest examples of the deranged semantic analysis often practiced by Brockelmann. The expression ܚܦܝܬܘܬܐ ܙܗܝܬܐ means "exceptional diligence"; there is nothing careful or wary about it other than what comes from the diligence of the head noun. This all-too-common practice in LS2 is very frustrating for the lexicographer and it is a shame that Sokoloff made few efforts to eliminate it.

entries.form_section: So in the Pesh.; read rather: zwēḏnē; see note.
entries.end_notes: Base is the traditional interpretation of the Peshitta term at Gen. 6:16 and, hence, how it is used in subsequent literature. In its original context, however, it clearly is to be interpreted as following the Jewish interpretive tradition as an opening for light at the top of the ark. Hence, it is beyond any doubt a loanword from the quite common Akkadian architectural term samētu [pronounced something like /zwēd/ in Assyrian], "CAD (s.v. samītu): battlemented parapet," but probably better something like "ornamented protrusion of a wall".

=Greek ὕσωππος. The annotations of the native Syriac lexicographers (see the extensive quotations in PSm) make it clear that this word was no longer in active use in post-biblical times. Note that it appears to be an Aramaic loan in Neo-Babylonian (CAD Z p. 163; contrast G. Bos, European Journal of Jewish Studies 2.2(2009): 349).
Given Hebrew אֵזוֹב, the form appearing in Jewish Aramaic texts, the precise route of this eastern Mediterranean culture word cannot be determined. See note s.v. ˀyzwb.

entries.end_notes: From the native lexicographers interpreted as a corrupt form from Greek ὑπόρριν, i.e. lit. "what is under the nose”, hence “mustache”. However since the only actual text found so far with the word clearly has the meaning “tooth” of a key,” it is probable that the Arabic homograph سبلة has been misinterpreted as “mustache” instead of “spike”. Thus the word should be compared to the lexical variant suparinnu of the more common Akkadian supinnu “trowel” or the like, the same as Aramaic swpyn, i..e, “sharp point”. All of these forms belong to the non-Semitic stratum of cultural objects ending in -innu; see AIOA f.n. 324.
See Wright's note.

Panaino has recently argued instead for an Old Iranian origin in Iran and the Caucasus 21(2017): 150ff.; however his understanding of the phonology and semantics of the Semitic material is weak. entries.end_notes: Only in lexical sources, where the diacritics are quite uncertainly cited.  Clearly, though, a device for working on livestock.

This word probably illustrates the most complicated and multi-faceted set of developments in all of Aramaic (or for that matter within the Semitic languages). It would appear that sqwṭ and its phonetic by-form ( when there is no intermediate vowel) zqwṭ are dissimilated forms of *zṭwṭ > sṭwṭ-- the latter a diminuitive from zwṭˀ which is a simplification of zwṭr which is a by-form or parallel development of the common Semitic root that appears most frequently in Aramaic as zˁr, "to be small."

In JLA only in the expression קומה זקופה, almost certainly a Hebraism.
The cited LJLA example is probably better understood to be a hypercorrection of sqwp "lintel, threshold" used figuratively, i.e., "portal", but see also s.v. zqwp v.n.D : "refutation, denial".
